narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.90—well above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 40 that stands out compared to the usual 36.70, you've built an impressive academic foundation heading into law school. Your family comes from a lower-middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 92% of students in this dataset. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 43.9%, tipping the scales toward rejection to minimize the chance of future struggles. This prediction hinges on just a few key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on the outcome. The most impactful was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, far below the average of 5.53, which strongly signaled to the model a heightened risk of bar exam failure by highlighting a dip in relative performance among peers. Pushing strongly in the opposite direction—toward predicting success—was your standout LSAT score, which reassured the model of your potential despite other concerns. Your high undergraduate GPA also worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward passing by demonstrating consistent excellence from your prior studies. While your early Year 1 decile of 3 was below average too, it played a lesser role here compared to these top influences. Overall, your situation compares to typical students by excelling in entrance metrics like LSAT and GPA, but the model weighed your weaker school deciles—especially later on—as a red flag not commonly seen in approved profiles. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school rankings, as even small improvements there could shift future predictions dramatically. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early or explore part-time options if they align better with building study habits. With your strong baseline, persistence and addressing those decile gaps could open doors at other schools soon.
